event_blocks.summary: At an auto show, a black Brabus 900 sedan, emblazoned with 'BRABUS' and '900', is presented as a testament to 'Deluxe tuning made in Germany' from the heart of the Ruhr Valley, where Brabus transforms Mercedes-Benz vehicles into 'driving powerhouses' with 'more displacement, more horsepower, more torque.' Inside the modern Brabus facility, a white Brabus G-Wagen with its hood open reveals a powerful engine as a technician works nearby, preparing for a speed test aiming for 300 kilometers per hour with its '900 horsepower,' emphasizing 'Performance tuning at any cost.' Another black Brabus G-Wagen undergoes work, with mechanics attending to its engine, noting the vehicles can be valued at 'half a million or even possibly one million euros.' The black Brabus sedan is then subjected to intense performance testing on a dynamometer, marked by loud engine revving, tire squeal, and acceleration sounds, while a technician monitors. The narrator highlights Brabus's 'more than 40 years' of 'supreme precision work.' A man in a Brabus vest speaks, stressing the need for accuracy: 'You can't be off by even one or two millimeters.' The workshop showcases the development and manufacturing of new components using 'state-of-the-art technology,' including a close-up of a large yellow brake caliper being installed, a mechanic using a power tool on a car's interior, a technician employing a 3D scanner on a car part, and a hand sanding a white car part in a spray booth. The final scenes depict a mechanic with a tattooed arm working on an engine and a wide shot of the high-tech workshop filled with CNC machines and tools, underscoring precision engineering, culminating in a close-up of a hand adjusting an interior component, with the exclamation 'Toys for the boys' echoing the meticulous craftsmanship.

Spray Booth.description: A clean, white room where a technician sands a car part, likely preparing it for painting or finishing.

Brabus Facility.description: A modern building with a large 'BRABUS' sign, housing a spacious workshop with multiple vehicles and advanced equipment, described as being 'at the heart of the Ruhr Valley' where 'supreme precision work' has been performed for 'more than 40 years.'

Dynamometer.description: A performance testing machine with a black sedan mounted on it, used to measure engine output and vehicle performance, accompanied by intense engine revving, tire squeal, and powerful acceleration sounds, demonstrating the vehicle's '900 horsepower' and potential to reach '300 kilometers an hour.'

CNC Machines.description: Large industrial machines in the workshop, used for precision manufacturing of custom car components.

Brabus G-Wagen.description: A black and white armored SUV with a distinctive Brabus front grille and 'B' logo, shown both in the workshop and on the road.

Brabus 900 Sedan.description: A black luxury sedan with a prominent Brabus grille and 'BRABUS 900' badge, displayed at an auto show and later tested on a dynamometer, associated with achieving '300 kilometers an hour' and '900 horsepower,' and generating intense engine revving, tire squeal, and powerful acceleration sounds.

event_blocks.summary: Inside the Brabus Facility in Bottrop, a CNC machine meticulously machines a complex engine block, a process the narrator describes as fine-tuning on a nano scale, while a technician in a black shirt inspects a large engine component, demonstrating specialized expertise. The facility, identified as the tuning premises from the Ruhr Valley, houses a vast parking lot filled with luxury vehicles, notably numerous Brabus-modified Mercedes-Benz models. A man in a black Brabus puffer jacket inspects a white Brabus G-Wagen, removing protective coverings before entering and driving it through the lot, passing other high-end models. Upon arrival, new base vehicles undergo a quality inspection for transit damage, as the man proceeds to drive the G-Wagen into a SoftCare car wash tunnel. The vehicle is automatically washed with spraying water and soap, a crucial step as the narrator explains dirt is taboo in the garages to prevent impurities from affecting high-performance tuning parts, though he mistakenly refers to the vehicle as a Maybach. Emerging clean and gleaming from the wash, the Brabus G-Wagen is ready for its next phase, accompanied by upbeat, industrial-sounding background music.

event_blocks.summary: A black luxury sedan entered the Brabus facility, a 150,000 square meter workshop where approximately 6,500 vehicles are converted annually, revealing numerous Brabus G-Wagens and customized models. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, explained that performance upgrades cost €1,590 plus VAT, adding 20-30 horsepower in a couple of hours, while complete overhauls take three to six months, resulting in vehicles worth up to one million euros with up to 900 horsepower. The scene shifted to the LEITSTAND control room, a central hub where team leaders and workshop managers, including Gramm, reviewed a Whiteboard Task System featuring task cards and red dots, managing around 150 global customer wish lists. Steffen Schnur, Workshop Manager, demonstrated the workflow on the whiteboard, detailing a full conversion of a G63 Smart Ultimate with a starry sky, full leather interior, and complete exterior remodeling, including performance boosts, valve-controlled exhausts, and specific aesthetic requests like no chrome or silver details. The workshop displayed rows of Brabus G-Wagens, with the Brabus 800 Widestar identified as a favorite model, noting its continuous refinement in Bottrop for nearly four decades, with the 2.5-ton off-roader now upgradeable to 900 horsepower. Dynamic shots showed a black Brabus G-Wagen on a mountain road, followed by a CGI animation of a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 on a virtual platform, confirming it as the base vehicle with specifications of V_max 220 km/h and 585 HP, highlighting Brabus's precision engineering and luxury performance through a blend of real-world operations, driving footage, and digital visualization.

Brabus Facility.description: A modern, spacious workshop with bright lighting, multiple service bays, and high-end vehicles including Brabus G-Wagens and sedans, some on lifts, with a 'LEITSTAND' control room visible on an upper level, where around 6,500 vehicles are converted annually across 150,000 square meters.

The car enters a spacious, well-lit workshop, where a narrator explains that around 6,500 vehicles are converted annually across 150,000 square meters. The camera pans across the clean, organized environment, revealing numerous high-end vehicles, including multiple Brabus G-Wagens and other customized models, some elevated on lifts. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, appears in a black Brabus-branded puffer vest, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He details the customization options, stating that a performance upgrade costs €1,590 plus VAT, adding 20-30 horsepower in a couple of hours, while a complete overhaul can take three to six months, resulting in a vehicle worth up to one million euros with up to 900 horsepower. The scene then transitions to an elevated 'LEITSTAND' (Control Stand) control room, where a group of men, including Sven Gramm, gather around a large whiteboard covered in task cards and red dots. A narrator explains this is the car tuner's control center, where team leaders and workshop managers meet daily to discuss production status, with around 150 wish lists from car owners worldwide pinned to the wall, emphasizing the global demand for Brabus's personalized vehicles. Gramm points to specific cards, illustrating the workflow, as the team observes attentively, highlighting the professional and coordinated atmosphere.

The video opens with a close-up of a large whiteboard, a 'Whiteboard Task System' covered in 'Arbeitskarte' task cards with red circular markers, organized into columns like 'Kunden Aufträge' and 'Auslieferungstermin 2–6 Wochen'. Steffen Schnur, the Workshop Manager, in a white shirt, gestures and moves these cards, explaining the workflow in German, translated as "We're creating a starry sky for our customers in a G63 Smart Ultimate," detailing a "full leather interior with a complete exterior remodeling." He elaborates on performance upgrades like a "performance boost in kit and a valve controlled exhaust," mentioning some customers desire "700 horsepower, speedometer, roof instruments, door sill panels, and a carbon bonnet" for a "full conversion," and even specific requests like "no chrome or silver details... or any emblems or logos." The camera follows his hand movements, emphasizing the structured process, before transitioning to a row of Brabus G-Wagens, including black and beige models, parked in a clean, well-lit workshop, showcasing their rugged design and distinctive front grilles. A narrator's voice identifies "The customer's favorite model is the Brabus 800 Widestar," noting that "for nearly four decades, the off-road vehicle has been continually fine-tuned in Bottrop," and that this "2.5-ton off-roader can now be upgraded to 900 horsepower." The scene then cuts to dynamic shots of a black Brabus G-Wagen driving on a winding mountain road, highlighting its performance and aggressive styling. This is followed by a CGI animation of a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 rotating on a virtual platform, with the narrator confirming "The Mercedes-AMG G63 forms the base," and on-screen text displaying its specifications: 'V_max 220 km/h' and 'Power: 585 HP', emphasizing its high-performance capabilities, with the narrator reiterating "Top speed of 220 km/h." The sequence blends real-world workshop operations with high-octane driving and digital visualization, all set to background music, to convey Brabus’s precision engineering and luxury performance.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with CGI animations showcasing a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 with 585 HP and 850 Nm of torque, followed by a black Brabus G-Wagen boasting 800 HP, 1000 Nm of torque, and a 0-100 km/h time of 4.1 seconds, accompanied by engine revving and whoosh sounds. Transitioning to the Brabus facility, 'factory one,' an aerial view reveals a large parking lot and a 'BRABUS' sign, with the narrator noting a production vehicle's €150,000 base price doubling after conversion. Inside the workshop, a silver Mercedes-AMG G63, tagged '240' on the windshield, is raised on a hydraulic lift by a technician in a black shirt operating the control panel. The technician, wearing blue gloves, removes the standard exhaust system and unpacks a new, chrome-plated sport exhaust from a cardboard box, installing dual black-tipped pipes, which the narrator explains is for aesthetics and sound, not speed. The scene then focuses on mechanics, including Samir Boulahya, installing the high-performance exhaust under the G63 on a 'Hebebühne 4' hydraulic lift. Samir, speaking German, highlights the improved design and sound, demonstrating a 'sport exhaust' button that activates 'sport mode' for a great sound. After adjusting the lift's height via a gray control panel, Samir prepares for a test drive, anticipating the results. Thirty minutes later, with the valve-controlled exhaust fitted and adding €7,000 to the car's value, a sound check commences. The car's engine is heard in normal mode before Samir activates the new component, unleashing a significantly louder and more aggressive roar, eliciting a satisfied 'Toys for the boys' from the mechanic.

event_blocks.summary: At the Brabus Facility, a silver Mercedes-AMG G63 undergoes customization, with a technician installing a new exhaust system, one pipe covered by a blue plastic bag, while the narrator notes European regulations limit vehicle noise to 75 decibels. Inside the G63, Samir Boulahya, wearing a Brabus shirt, adjusts the steering wheel and examines the dashboard featuring blue ambient lighting and a partially disassembled center console. The G63 is then raised on a white hydraulic lift, 'Hebebühne 4', its hood open. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, in a Brabus vest, assures viewers that Brabus complies with regulations, citing safety concerns as a reason to refuse requests for excessively loud vehicles or gold airbag logos. An aerial view reveals the spacious, well-lit Brabus Facility workshop, populated with multiple Brabus G-Wagens, predominantly black with one beige, in various states of modification. Fitters in the glass hall are replacing original paneling and aerodynamic parts with a 'wide star kit', increasing the SUV's value significantly. A technician in a Brabus shirt inspects a black G-Wagen with red-rimmed wheels, while another works on a beige G-Wagen bearing a '560' tag. The segment highlights a black G-Wagen's carbon fiber bonnet, identified as the 'Powerdome', emphasizing the high-performance customization. A CGI animation of a black Brabus G-Wagen showcases its aggressive features, including large air ducts in the front spoiler for engine cooling and extended wings for a beefier look, alongside '23 INCH LIGHT ALLOY RIMS' with red brake calipers and a 'STAINLESS STEEL EXHAUST' with a throttle system for a powerful sound. In the workshop, two Technicians in Black Shirts meticulously assemble a front bumper component, part of a 'full conversion kit' of approximately 50 pre-painted individual parts, using a power drill. They prepare and align the bumper for installation on a black Brabus G-Wagen, which will be widened by 10 cm on each side to accommodate larger tires, with other customized Brabus G-Wagens visible in the background, including one with a '329' tag.

The narrator details its aggressive features, explaining how 'large air ducts' in the newly installed front spoiler provide additional oxygen for the tuned engine, while 'extended wings' on the front and rear axle give it a 'beefier look'. On-screen text highlights these modifications, including '23 INCH LIGHT ALLOY RIMS' with red brake calipers and a 'STAINLESS STEEL EXHAUST' with a throttle system, which the narrator states provides its 'powerful sound'. The scene transitions to a well-lit, spacious Brabus workshop where two Technicians in Black Shirts are seen meticulously assembling a front bumper component on a workbench, using a power drill. The narrator identifies them as 'the mechanics' and 'employees' who are 'assembling the new wings' as part of a 'full conversion kit' consisting of 'around 50 individual parts', all pre-painted. They carefully prepare and align the bumper for installation on a black Brabus G-Wagen, which, as the narrator explains, will become '10 cm wider on either side' to accommodate 'chunky tires'. Multiple other Brabus G-Wagens in various stages of customization are visible in the background, including one with a '329' tag.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a detailed view of an Alloy Wheel, reflecting the Warehouse Environment, before showing Markus Oczyllok, a Technician in Black Shirt, walking through the warehouse filled with tires and Cardboard Box. Markus retrieves a large cardboard box and a silver multi-spoke alloy wheel, described as machined from a single piece, and carries it to a blue and black tire mounting machine. He then rolls a new black 'YOKOHAMA' Tire to the machine, noting the sports tires' importance for a two-and-a-half-ton car at speeds up to 240 km/h. Markus mounts the tire onto the wheel using the Tire Mounting Machine's mechanical arms, emphasizing the precision required to avoid disastrous consequences. He inflates the tire, checks its pressure, and ensures proper seating, while explaining the process and cautioning, "Das kann... It could get dangerous then. I wouldn't like to be sitting in the car if a tire bursts."

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a glossy black car wheel, reflecting the workshop environment, before transitioning to a wide shot of a spacious, well-lit warehouse-like space filled with stacked tires and boxes, marked with safety signage and industrial equipment. The audio notes that "The new 23-inch wheels are already waiting in the warehouse next door." Markus retrieves a large cardboard box, then a silver multi-spoke alloy wheel, which the audio describes as "huge alloy wheels, machined from a single piece." He carries the wheel to a blue and black 'easymount pro' tire mounting machine, carefully placing it on the machine’s hub. He then retrieves a new black 'YOKOHAMA' tire, rolling it to the machine. The audio explains that these "sports tires will later have to keep the two and a half ton car on the road at speeds of up to 240 kilometers per hour," with "the casing, the central framework of the tire, and a special rubber compound" ensuring "the necessary traction." Markus mounts the tire onto the wheel using the machine’s mechanical arms, with close-ups showing the tire being pressed onto the rim and the machine’s clamps securing it. The consequences would be disastrous." Markus then inflates the tire, checks its pressure, and ensures it is properly seated. Throughout, the clean, organized, and technical workshop environment is visible. Markus, with a focused and professional demeanor, speaks directly to the camera, explaining the process and adding, "Das kann... It could get dangerous then. I wouldn't like to be sitting in the car if a tire bursts."

event_blocks.summary: In a Brabus Facility, a Technician in Black Shirt works on a Brabus G-Wagen's engine bay on a Hydraulic Lift while another installs a Carbon Bonnet, with a narrator explaining Brabus tunes Mercedes-Benz cars, exporting over 85% globally, primarily to Asia, the USA, Russia, and the Gulf region, accompanied by driving orchestral music. A mechanic tightens components under the car with a wrench, emphasizing precision. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, wearing a black Brabus-branded puffer vest, speaks to the camera, stating that their international clientele includes chief executives, royalty, Formula 1 champions, actors, and pop stars, confirming celebrity ownership as daily business, with subtle workshop sounds audible. An aerial view shows the modern Brabus Facility and its expansive parking lot. Inside, a Technician in White Shirt operates a 3D Scanner on a Brabus 900 Sedan mounted on a dynamometer, adjusting the scanner and retrieving a power tool. The narrator details that new vehicle part construction is a daily task under strict secrecy, with initial development in the 'catacombs' where a design engineer prepares the 3D scanner for new models within the clean, professional facility. Kai Brekoff from Development, wearing a white Brabus-branded shirt, operates a handheld FARO 3D scanner, emitting a blue laser line over a glossy black Brabus G-Wagen's front grille and hood, as the narrator explains that existing kits often don't fit new models, necessitating new attachments. The scanner, connected to a Laptop displaying real-time 3D point cloud data, generates a digital model for new designs. Kai explains the process of creating an optical line with dots and coupled motion to get a flat surface, and how a light beam is emitted and recaptured by a receiver camera to obtain correct distances, all underscored by upbeat electronic music. The narrator concludes by emphasizing the 3D scanner's precise measurement of vehicle contours, highlighting Brabus's advanced technology in automotive customization and quality control.

raw_segments: The video opens with Markus Oczyllok, a Tyre Mechanic wearing a black Brabus-branded shirt, meticulously mounting a new black Yokohama tire onto a silver multi-spoke Alloy Wheel using a large blue and black 'easymount pro' Tire Mounting Machine. Throughout this process, a male narrator explains that Brabus vehicles are faster due to special materials and precision work, noting that a set of these 'super tires' can cost up to 17,000 euros, equivalent to a new small car. Close-ups show Markus's hands adjusting the tire valve and using a pressure gauge before he removes the completed wheel and places it on a wooden pallet in a well-lit Warehouse Environment. The narrator then introduces the next crucial step, the 'balancing machine,' explaining that for perfect rotation on the road, machine sensors measure any imbalance on the axle, displaying the location to the technician. A 'fitter,' like Markus, then offsets the slightest deviation by attaching weights to the rim, a method by which a 'tire specialist' assembles around 50 wheels daily. The scene transitions to a black Brabus G-Wagen elevated on a Hydraulic Lift in a clean workshop, its hood open to reveal a high-performance engine. The narrator emphasizes that a 'fast car also needs powerful brakes,' as the camera focuses on the vehicle’s large, drilled brake disc and a red Brabus-branded Brake Caliper. The video then cuts to a table displaying multiple Brake Calipers in various colors—red, gold, and orange—while the narrator remarks that the 'original red of the disc brake caliper does not appeal to everyone,' highlighting customization options. Finally, a technician in a black Brabus-branded shirt and gloves is seen using a hammer to work on a part on a workbench, in a bright, industrial setting, emphasizing precision and customization, all set to a continuous background music track.

Against a backdrop of upbeat, electronic music, the segment opens with a close-up of car mechanic Samir Boulahya, identified by on-screen text, meticulously working on a yellow Brabus-branded brake caliper on a workbench. Wearing a black Brabus shirt with red 'ADVAN MOTUL' logos and grey Maxiflex gloves, he uses a hammer to precisely adjust a component, embodying the narrator's description of brake remounting as a 'highly sensitive task' with 'error tolerance 0.0' for high-end tuners. Samir then turns to the camera, emphasizing the critical nature of brakes: "Yeah, this is the most important component in a vehicle. No brakes means no way of stopping. And the customer could be killed. You cannot be off by one or two millimeters. It's truly precision work," with the German phrase 'Präzisionsarbeit' audible. He moves to a black Brabus G-Wagen, elevated on a 'Hebebühne 4' hydraulic lift, and painstakingly installs the yellow brake caliper onto the large, drilled brake disc, using a torque wrench to secure it with audible clicks and clanks. The narrator highlights this as the mechanic taking his time, contrasting 'custom workshop' with 'assembly line work' where 'stress could increase the error rate.' Samir then inspects a red brake caliper on the clean, well-lit workbench, while his voiceover reflects on past jobs: "Where I used to work, the jobs were all on tight schedules. Slaving away non-stop. They would use a stopwatch to see if we could get the job done in the set time. What matters here, everything has to be top-notch," reinforcing the meticulous standards of the Brabus facility, where other Brabus vehicles are visible in the background.

The video opens with Samir Boulahya, a car mechanic in a black Brabus-branded shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit workshop, as a calm, authoritative male narrator emphasizes the need for "100% quality, precision, and safety" when tuning "powerhouses." The scene transitions to a black Brabus G-Wagen elevated on a white industrial hydraulic lift, where Samir meticulously installs a large yellow Brabus brake caliper onto a drilled brake disc, using a torque wrench and hammer on a metal workbench, accompanied by the distinct sound of a hammer striking metal. An upbeat, driving electronic music track begins. An aerial view of the modern Brabus Facility in Bottrop is shown, followed by technicians in black Brabus shirts inside a "large glass hall," manually assembling a black front bumper with blue protective tape. The narrator explains that "mechanics" here "modify four SUVs every week," giving them "the beast look." Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, then speaks in German, translated by the narrator, about customers seeking "custom-made cars" that "stand out from the crowd." The technicians finalize the bumper installation on the G-Wagen, highlighting its aggressive grille and headlights. The narrator adds that a "450 strong staff" in Bottrop meets demanding customer requirements. The video concludes with a white Brabus G-Wagen with a camouflage wrap and a black Brabus G-Wagen exiting the facility, followed by a black Brabus 900 Sedan on a dynamometer, where an engine revving sound underscores its performance testing, as the narrator mentions Brabus's global presence with "2,500 employees" in "more than 100 countries."

The video opens inside the modern, spacious Brabus Facility, where technicians are actively assembling and modifying high-performance vehicles. A Technician in Black Shirt works on the engine bay of a Brabus G-Wagen, elevated on a Hydraulic Lift, while another technician installs a Carbon Bonnet. A clear, professional male narrator explains that Brabus specializes in tuning Mercedes-Benz cars, with over 85% of their customized vehicles exported globally, primarily to Asia, the USA, Russia, and the Gulf region, all set to a backdrop of driving, modern orchestral music. The scene shifts to a close-up of a mechanic tightening components under the car with a wrench, emphasizing precision. Sven Gramm, Director of Communications, then appears in the workshop, wearing a black Brabus-branded puffer vest, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. With a confident German voice, translated by a professional male voice-over, he reveals their work for an international clientele including chief executives, royalty, Formula 1 champions, actors, and pop stars, confirming that top celebrities own their vehicles, which is their daily business. Subtle workshop sounds are audible beneath the music and speech. An aerial view of the Brabus Facility showcases its modern architecture and expansive parking lot. Back inside, a Technician in White Shirt operates a 3D Scanner, moving it toward a Brabus 900 Sedan mounted on a dynamometer, carefully adjusting the scanner and retrieving a power tool. The narrator's voice returns, detailing that the construction of new vehicle parts is a daily task, with strict secrecy applied, and initial development stages occurring in the 'catacombs' of the plant, where a design engineer prepares the ultra-modern 3D scanner for new vehicle models, all within the clean, well-lit, and professional high-end facility.

The video opens in a clean, well-lit Brabus Facility, where Kai Brekoff from the Development Department, wearing a white Brabus-branded shirt with sponsor logos, meticulously operates a handheld FARO 3D scanner. As he maneuvers the scanner, emitting a blue laser line over the front grille and hood of a glossy black Brabus G-Wagen, a calm male narrator explains that existing spoiler, bumper, or wing kits often don't fit new models, necessitating the design of new attachments. The scanner, connected to a laptop displaying real-time 3D point cloud data with green and blue sections, is crucial for this, as the narrator details how it generates a digital model of relevant vehicle parts to serve as a basis for new designs. Kai occasionally pauses to speak to the camera, gesturing towards the equipment, and with a clear German accent, he elaborates on the technical process: "This large number of dots in a row creates an optical line, and with the coupled motion, we visually get a flat surface." He continues, explaining that a light beam is emitted and recaptured by a receiver camera, allowing them to obtain the correct distances of the scanned data. Throughout, an upbeat electronic background music plays, underscoring the high-tech environment. The narrator concludes by emphasizing that the 3D scanner precisely measures the vehicle contours, highlighting the advanced technology used in Brabus's automotive customization and quality control.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a Brabus Facility workshop, a Technician in a white Brabus-branded shirt operates a FARO 3D scanner with a carbon fiber extension arm, projecting a blue laser line to capture a precise digital image of the front of a black Brabus G-Wagen, elevated on a hydraulic lift, for the design of a new front section; real-time data, including a 3D point cloud model and part number '18782446', is visible on a laptop screen, with the narrator noting that scanning a complete vehicle would take up to two working days as upbeat electronic music begins. The scene shifts to a modern office where the Technician, now at a desk with dual monitors, uses 3D CAD software and a 3D mouse to process the new 3D data of the front spoiler, manipulating a detailed model of a car's front bumper and grille featuring the Brabus logo, while a photorealistic rendering of a silver Brabus sedan is displayed; he is heard saying in German, "Hier ist dann der Anschluss" (Here is the connection), gesturing toward the screen to explain design elements, as other team members, including a colleague, work at adjacent desks. The narrator emphasizes that new spoilers and air intakes must fit the vehicle, considering material properties and safety aspects, and highlights the challenges of manufacturing carbon fiber components while complying with TÜV and statutory regulations for product authorization.

event_blocks.summary: In a bright, modern office, a designer refines a silver Brabus sedan model using 3D CAD software and a 3D mouse, while a narrator explains Brabus's history of custom parts developed in Factory 2. Inside the Brabus Facility, a technician operates a 3D printer to create white plastic roof instrument holders, then cleans them in a powder removal chamber with a sodium hydroxide solution. Another technician uses a silicone mold to cast parts into rubber molds on a rotating platform, a process that begins with creating a silicon mold from sanitized prototypes to cast future components. The workshop features a white metal shelving unit storing approximately 500 brown rubber molds labeled with vehicle classes. A technician retrieves a mold, removes a gray 3D-printed component, and uses a red heat gun to smooth its surface before placing it into an industrial curing oven set to 72.4°C for 280 minutes. The mold is then sealed with a pneumatic stapler before casting. A gloved hand prepares a two-component compound by pouring a hardener from a gray bottle into a white bucket on a digital scale. This mixture is poured into a white rectangular mold inside a vacuum casting machine/curing oven. A materials engineer later removes a cured, yellowish component from a black tray, fits it with filler hoses, and places it into another casting machine, with the process emphasizing dimensional accuracy and avoiding air pockets.

raw_segments: The video opens in a bright, modern office where a designer in a white Brabus-branded shirt intently refines a photorealistic silver Brabus sedan model using 3D CAD software on dual monitors and a black 3D mouse with a blue illuminated ring. A 'LANDFELD Atlas 8' manual and colored markers are on his desk, reflecting a creative and technical workspace. A narrator explains that "Over the course of the company's 40-plus year history, the Bottrop designers have developed thousands of custom parts," as cinematic, orchestral music begins to build. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the expansive Brabus Facility, a large, modern industrial complex surrounded by greenery, as the narrator adds that "The new components see the light of day in Factory 2." Inside, a technician, identified by the narrator as a 'model builder,' in a black Brabus 'TEAM' shirt operates a 3D printer, which whirs as it prints white plastic components, specifically "three newly designed roof instrument holders." He then carefully removes the freshly printed parts and, wearing a green protective glove, places them into a powder removal chamber, cleaning them with a brush, accompanied by cleaning sounds, as the narrator states he "cleans the blanks thoroughly with a sodium hydroxide solution." The process continues with another technician, also referred to as a 'model builder,' in a white apron, handling a blue silicone mold to remove a 3D-printed part before placing it into a brown rubber mold on a rotating platform. The narrator clarifies that "Using the sanitized prototypes, the model builders then construct a mold out of silicon," which "is used to cast all future roof instrument holders." The final shot reveals a well-organized shelf filled with various molds, labeled for vehicle classes like 'C-Klasse / E-Klasse', underscoring the systematic approach to custom part production, all set against the continuing orchestral score.

The video opens in a clean, well-lit workshop, showcasing a white metal shelving unit, identified as part of the 'plastics production stores,' labeled with vehicle classes like 'C-Klasse / E-Klasse' and 'GLS / GLA / GT,' and filled with approximately '500 of the silicon blocks' (brown rubber molds). Industrial music plays as a technician, one of the '10 employees' in the workshop, wearing a white Brabus-branded shirt, retrieves a large brown 'silicon block' (rubber mold) from the shelf and places it on a rotating workbench. He carefully removes a gray 3D-printed component, revealing its intricate shape, highlighting that even the 'smallest plastic pieces are handcrafted.' The scene shifts to another shelving unit with various gray 3D-printed parts. The technician then uses a red heat gun to smooth the component's surface, demonstrating precision. He places the part into a large industrial curing oven, which displays 72.4°C and a 280-minute timer, aligning with the audio's mention of storing 'the silicon block' in a 'curing oven' at 70°C to allow liquid plastic to spread better. The audio then describes the process of sealing 'the mold for the roof instrument holders' tightly with a pneumatic stapler 'before casting' to prevent liquid plastic from escaping, an action performed by 'the worker.' Finally, a gloved hand pours a clear liquid into a white bucket, as the audio explains 'the worker is now preparing the two-component compound' for the next manufacturing stage. The overall environment, with its industrial equipment, underscores a high-precision, controlled manufacturing process for custom automotive parts.

The segment opens with a technician, identified by the narrator as a materials engineer, in a white Brabus-branded shirt and white latex gloves, meticulously pouring a dark, viscous liquid, described by the narrator as a transparent hardener, from a gray bottle into a white bucket positioned on a digital scale for precise measurement. The narrator explains that this hardener, along with a second component, is responsible for the color of the eventual roof instrument holder, emphasizing the need for precise composition of both elements. The white bucket is then transferred into a large, stainless steel 'BRABUS' industrial oven, which the narrator refers to as a vacuum casting machine and curing oven, displaying a temperature of 72.4°C and a timer set for 280 minutes. After the technician operates the control panel, a close-up inside the oven shows the bucket mechanically tilting to pour the liquid into a white rectangular mold, where the two components bind to form polyurethane. Later, the materials engineer removes a cured, yellowish component with a white top, now identified by the narrator as a silicon block and part of the roof instrument holder, from a black tray. He inspects it before fitting it with three filler hoses and placing it into another casting machine. The narrator notes that the black resin, likely referring to the dark liquid, provides excellent dimensional accuracy for high-quality molded parts, and the overall process aims to avoid abnormal cavities caused by air pockets. Throughout the precise manufacturing process in the well-lit workshop, a continuous background music track plays.

event_blocks.summary: Within the Brabus Facility, a technician in a black shirt, wearing blue nitrile gloves, meticulously positions a large aluminum engine block onto a Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine, securing it with hex bolts. A torque plate is fitted to simulate engine assembly tension, preventing warping, before a specialized honing tool with diamond bars is attached. As the Brabus-labeled machine activates, the honing tool is lowered into a cylinder bore, spraying coolant while rotating and moving axially to smooth the interior to within hundredths of a millimeter, creating a perfect cylindrical surface. Thomas Schreiber monitors and adjusts the digital control panel, ensuring the process, which enlarges the engine block's cylinders from 6 to 7.3 liters, results in optimal performance. The workshop, characterized by its clean, well-lit environment, industrial equipment, blue-handled carts, and workbenches, underscores the high-precision manufacturing and technical expertise involved in creating custom Brabus engine components, including the machining of prototype, standard, and development parts like crankcases and shims by Kai Baumann and his team.

Brabus Facility.description: A large, modern industrial complex with a white roof and gray walls, surrounded by greenery, shown from an aerial perspective, emphasizing its scale and clean design. The narrator describes it as 'The 150,000 square meter tuning premises'.

Yellowish Cured Component.description: A yellowish, rigid component with a white top, removed from a black tray after curing, inspected by the technician, and later placed on a wooden board for further processing. The narrator refers to it as a 'silicon block' that is 'opened' from the curing oven, and later as 'freshly molded vehicle parts'.

Black Tray.description: A black rectangular tray used to hold the yellowish cured component, placed on a wooden board and handled by the technician during the curing and inspection process.

CNC Machines.description: Large industrial machines actively machining the engine block, performing turning, milling, and honing procedures with utmost precision. They feature transparent doors, control panels, and coolant systems for precision cutting, and are loaded with milling cutters costing up to 3,000 Euros. The machines work for one and a half hours on the engine block's 12 cylinders, with the sounds of their operation audible throughout.

Heat Gun.description: A red handheld heat gun used by the technician to smooth the surface of a 3D-printed part, indicating a finishing step in the manufacturing process, accompanied by its distinct whooshing/hissing sound.

Rubber Mold.description: A large brown, rigid mold with a complex cavity, placed on a rotating platform by the technician, used to hold or shape parts during the manufacturing process. The narrator states 'The casting process takes 15 minutes' and that the 'mold is no longer usable' after 20-80 repetitions.

Technician in White Shirt.description: A man in a white Brabus-branded shirt with 'TEAM' on the sleeve and 'BRABUS' on the back, wearing white latex gloves, operating an industrial oven and handling a yellowish cured component, later working on a brown rubber mold and using a heat gun to finish a black conical part. The narrator refers to him as 'the employee' who 'may repeat this process between 20 to 80 times' and as one of 'the colleagues' applying finishing touches.

Industrial Oven.description: A large stainless steel oven labeled 'BRABUS' with a digital display showing temperature (72.4°C) and timer (280 minutes), featuring a transparent door and a control panel with buttons and dials, used to cure components. The narrator identifies it as a 'curing oven' where a 'silicon block' rests for '45 minutes'.

Kai Baumann.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos, speaking to the camera and supervising the CNC machining process, while a narrator describes the intricate engineering work.

Workbench.description: A metal workbench with a textured surface, holding various engine components, tools, and parts, including cylinder heads and custom metal pieces, organized for precision work.

Engine Block.description: A large, complex aluminum engine block with multiple cylinder bores, being machined on a Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine. The audio explains it is secured with hex bolts and a 'torque plate' to simulate full assembly tension, preventing warping when cylinder heads are mounted, ensuring perfect operation.

Technician in Black Shirt.description: A man in a black Brabus-branded shirt with 'BRABUS TEAM' on the back, operating the CNC machine’s control panel, adjusting settings, using a handheld probe to measure dimensions, and later handling the engine block with yellow gloves for precision, as the narrator describes the detailed machining steps.

Blue-Handled Cart.description: A mobile cart with blue metal handles, used to transport the machined engine block after processing, featuring a flat top surface and storage compartments.

Honing Tool.description: A specialized cylindrical tool attached to the Sunnen machine, described in the audio as having 'diamond bars.' It rotates and simultaneously moves in and out of the cylinder bore, smoothing the interior and removing 'hundredths of a millimeter' to achieve a perfect, cylindrical surface.

Blue Nitrile Gloves.description: A pair of blue disposable gloves worn by Thomas Schreiber, providing protection and precision during the handling of the engine block and honing tool.

Thomas Schreiber.description: A motor mechanic with short hair and glasses, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos on the sleeve, and blue nitrile gloves. He is actively operating the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine, securing the engine block with bolts and adjusting the honing tool. He speaks in a clear, informative voice, explaining the specialized equipment and the precision required for the honing process.

Hex Bolts.description: Silver hexagonal bolts used to secure the engine block to the honing machine. The audio explains these are part of a 'torque plate' setup, which simulates the tension of a fully assembled engine to prevent warping.

Coolant System.description: A system integrated into the Sunnen machine that sprays a liquid coolant onto the cylinder bore during honing to reduce friction and heat, visible as a dark liquid flowing over the block.

Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Mach.description: A white industrial machine with a digital control panel, labeled 'SUNNEN' and 'CK-21 BRABUS'. It features a vertical honing tool, coolant system, and a flexible black hose. The audio describes its function in detail, explaining how it uses a diamond-set honing tool to rotate and move in and out of the cylinder, removing 'hundredths of a millimeter' to create a perfect surface.

The technician uses a heat gun, whose distinct sound is heard, to smooth the edges of the part, applying finishing touches. The segment opens with a close-up of a large, complex engine block being precisely machined inside a CNC machine, with coolant spraying as the cutting tool operates, all within the "engine department" of "Factory 3" where "high-performance engines with up to 900 horsepower are created by hand." A technician in a black Brabus-branded shirt with 'TEAM' on the sleeve carefully monitors this process. The scene shifts to a wider view of the clean, well-lit, and organized workshop, revealing multiple CNC machines and workbenches filled with precision-engineered metal parts, including cylinder heads and custom components, highlighting that in this "mechanical production facility," "the necessary tuning parts are manufactured and finished by the employees." Kai Baumann, Manager CNC Processing, identified by on-screen text and wearing a black Brabus polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos, speaks to the camera, explaining that "prototype parts, standard parts, and development parts are processed and produced here," noting that "the largest part would be a crankcase and the smallest part a shim." He is later seen assisting another technician in loading a large aluminum engine block, specifically a "bare crankcase," into a milling machine, securing it with clamps and adjusting its position. The audio clarifies that "in order to equip engines with more displacement, the mechanics fix the bare crankcases into a milling machine," and that "this 12-cylinder engine will be used to power a limousine." Detailed shots capture the engine block’s internal structure, highlighting the precision of the machining process as "during the milling operation, the diameter of its cylinders are enlarged by reboring," which means "more fuel can ultimately be combusted and this means more power." The two technicians work collaboratively, with one operating the machine’s control panel while the other observes, surrounded by tools and parts neatly arranged on blue-handled carts and workbenches, showcasing the technical expertise and teamwork involved in CNC processing for custom Brabus engine components.

The video opens in a well-lit, organized workshop, where Kai Baumann, Manager CNC Processing, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, speaks directly to the camera. Behind him, another technician operates a CNC machine. A narrator explains the process, describing it as a purely mechanical operation involving turning and milling. The scene transitions to a close-up of a large aluminum engine block being precisely machined inside a CNC machine, with coolant spraying as the tool cuts the cylinder bores. The narrator details how engine builders use a 3D measuring sensor to determine the exact position of the workpiece, measuring each cylinder precisely to a thousandth of a millimeter, emphasizing that only utmost precision guarantees a perfect final result. A technician, identifiable by his 'BRABUS TEAM' shirt, operates the machine's control panel, adjusting settings, and then uses a handheld probe to measure the engine block's dimensions. The narrator mentions that a colleague loads the machine with milling cutters, which can cost up to 3,000 Euros. The machine continues its operation for one and a half hours, working through the 12 cylinders of the aluminum crankcase. This process enhances the engine block's displacement from 6 to 7.3 liters, an increase of slightly more than 20%. After machining, the component undergoes a honing procedure. The technician, now wearing yellow gloves, carefully removes the engine block from the machine and places it onto a blue-handled cart for transport, all within the clean and organized workshop environment. Throughout, the sounds of industrial machinery and the narrator's voice are present.

The segment opens with a close-up of a large aluminum engine block, featuring multiple cylinder bores, being handled by motor mechanic Thomas Schreiber, who wears blue nitrile gloves. He carefully positions the block on a Sunnen CK-21 Brabus cylinder honing machine, then secures it with hex bolts using a power tool. The audio explains that this process involves fitting a 'torque plate' which distorts the block as if it were fully assembled, simulating the tension that occurs when cylinder heads are mounted to prevent warping. Thomas then attaches a specialized honing tool, which he describes as 'another very special piece of equipment here' with 'diamond bars,' to the machine. As the machine, labeled with the Brabus logo and model name, is activated, the honing tool is lowered into a cylinder bore. Coolant sprays as the tool rotates and simultaneously moves in and out, smoothing the bore's interior. Thomas monitors the process closely, adjusting settings on the digital control panel, while the audio details that the machine removes 'hundredths of a millimeter' to create a perfect, cylindrical surface, ensuring everything works perfectly afterwards. The clean, well-lit workshop environment, with industrial equipment in the background, underscores the high-precision manufacturing setting and the meticulous technical expertise involved in engine building.

event_blocks.summary: In a Brabus workshop, Thomas Schreiber, wearing blue nitrile gloves, meticulously hones an aluminum engine block using the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Machine, its whirring and spraying sounds filling the air as coolant reduces friction and a hose extracts debris, a process critical for achieving the precise cross-grinding needed for oil adhesion and low-friction piston operation. Sven Bruckschlegel, Head of Engine Design, then inspects the honed cylinder bores with a borescope connected to a Sony monitor, examining the exposed silicon crystals within the aluminum-silicon alloy to ensure proper lubrication and prevent piston jamming. Meanwhile, other technicians weigh and mark precision-machined connecting rods and pistons to within a tenth of a gram, sorting them into matched pairs for balanced mass, crucial for smooth engine operation, a level of detail far exceeding standard production tolerances. The workshop's clean, industrial environment, with its white walls and tool cabinets, underscores the meticulous quality control. Further assembly involves a technician carefully inserting a bearing into a custom-designed crankshaft with a greater stroke than stock, highlighting the company's in-house development of components like the crankshaft and bi-turbo charger system, which features a 3D-printed metallic component and a turbine designed to compress air for increased power. The segment culminates with the black Brabus 900 Sedan undergoing performance testing on a chassis dynamometer, its engine revving loudly as the dashboard displays high-speed metrics and monitors show real-time torque and horsepower data, with technicians adjusting roller resistance to simulate various load scenarios, demonstrating the culmination of the engine's enhanced engineering.

Honing Tool.description: A specialized cylindrical tool attached to the Sunnen machine, used to smooth the interior of the engine’s cylinder bores, rotating and being lowered into the bore with coolant spraying during operation.

Blue Nitrile Gloves.description: A pair of blue disposable gloves worn by Thomas Schreiber, providing protection and precision during the handling of the engine block and honing tool.

Sony Monitor.description: A gray monitor with 'BRABUS' branding on the top bezel and 'SONY' on the bottom, displaying a magnified, blue-tinted view of a cylinder bore's textured interior during borescope inspection.

Thomas Schreiber.description: A motor mechanic with short hair and glasses, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos on the sleeve and blue nitrile gloves, actively operating the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Machine by securing the engine block with hex bolts and adjusting the honing tool.

Sven Bruckschlegel.description: Head of Engine Design, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, inspecting an engine block using a borescope connected to the Sony monitor, adjusting the tool and pointing to the screen to analyze the cylinder’s internal surface.

Borescope.description: A flexible inspection tool with a camera at the end, connected to the Sony monitor, used to visually inspect the internal surface of the engine’s cylinder bores, specifically to analyze the interaction between aluminum and silicon crystals and ensure proper silicon relief, as explained by the male voice.

Engine Block.description: A large, precision-machined aluminum 12-cylinder engine block with multiple cylinder bores, mounted on a white stand, being measured with a dial indicator and wiped clean by the technician, noted by the narrator to have an increased displacement of 1.3 liters due to a larger cylinder diameter and special crankshaft.

Hex Bolts.description: Silver hexagonal bolts used to secure the engine block to the honing machine, tightened with a power tool by Thomas Schreiber.

Workshop Environment.description: A clean, well-lit industrial workshop with white walls, tool cabinets, a large window showing greenery outside, and a professional atmosphere emphasizing precision engineering.

Coolant System.description: A system integrated into the Sunnen machine that sprays a liquid coolant onto the cylinder bore during honing to reduce friction and heat, visible as a dark liquid flowing over the block.

Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Mach.description: A white industrial machine with a digital control panel, labeled 'SUNNEN' and 'CK-21 BRABUS', featuring a vertical honing tool, coolant system, and a flexible black hose for debris extraction, actively used to hone the cylinder bores of the engine block, its operation accompanied by distinct whirring and spraying sounds, as a narrator explains its function in creating the necessary cross grinding for oil film adhesion and low-friction piston movement.

Connecting Rods and Pistons.description: Shiny, precision-machined connecting rods and pistons laid out in rows on a white workbench, surrounding the 3D-printed component, indicating preparation for assembly.

Digital Scale.description: A small, silver digital scale with a black display, used to weigh engine components like pistons, highlighted in the audio as part of the precise, low-tolerance manufacturing process.

Red Marker.description: A red marker used by the technicians to write identification numbers on the pistons and connecting rods, a step emphasized in the audio for ensuring traceability and quality control in a low-tolerance environment.

Technician in Black Shirt.description: A man in a black Brabus-branded polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos on the sleeve, wearing glasses, seen inspecting and assembling engine components, and later operating the vehicle on the dynamometer to alter the resistance of the rollers for testing.

Crankshaft.description: A large, polished metallic crankshaft mounted on a precision stand, being inspected and assembled. The audio extensively details its function ('converts the in and out motion of the pistons into torque') and highlights it as a custom-developed component with 'greater stroke as opposed to stock,' emphasizing its special nature from the tuning workshop.

Female Technician.description: A woman with short blond hair, wearing glasses and a black Brabus-branded polo shirt with orange 'BRABUS' logos on the sleeve, marking connecting rods with a red marker at a white workbench, participating in the detailed assembly process.

3D-Printed Metallic Component.description: A complex, lattice-textured metallic part with a central hub, being assembled by the technician, featuring a shiny, multi-bladed turbine attached to its center, described as part of a modified exhaust manifold designed to increase engine air intake and performance.

Workbench.description: A clean, white workbench with a textured surface, holding the 3D-printed component, turbine, and rows of connecting rods and pistons, organized for precision assembly.

Turbine Component.description: A shiny, multi-bladed metallic turbine with a circular housing, attached to the 3D-printed component, being rotated and inspected for alignment and finish, explicitly described as 'enlarged turbine wheels with specially shaped blades' that 'raise the efficiency of the turbocharger' for 'more power'.

Dial Indicator.description: A precision measuring tool with a yellow arm and a dial face, attached to the engine block to measure cylinder bore dimensions, with its probe moving across the surface.

Dynamometer.description: A performance testing machine with a black sedan mounted on it, used to measure engine output and vehicle performance, explicitly called the 'chassis dynamometer' where 'the technician alters the resistance of the rollers' to create different test scenarios.

Dashboard.description: The car's digital dashboard displaying performance metrics such as speed (up to 231 km/h), RPM (up to 1710), and boost pressure, with a yellow and black color scheme.

Brabus 900 Sedan.description: A black luxury sedan with a prominent Brabus grille and 'BRABUS 900' badge, displayed at an auto show and later tested on a dynamometer, referred to as 'the unit' whose performance is being tested under 'various load scenarios' and 'different gradients and slopes'.

Monitors.description: Two computer monitors in the testing facility displaying real-time performance data, including torque, horsepower, and engine speed, with German text such as 'Konstante Zugkraft' (Constant Traction).

raw_segments: The segment opens with Thomas Schreiber, a motor mechanic in a black Brabus-branded polo shirt and blue nitrile gloves, operating the Sunnen CK-21 Brabus Cylinder Honing Machine. The machine's distinct whirring and spraying sounds accompany him as he carefully secures a large aluminum engine block with hex bolts, then lowers the honing tool into a cylinder bore while coolant sprays to reduce friction, with a flexible black hose extracting debris. A narrator emphasizes the precision required, stating that "one mistake and the 15,000 euro engine block is ruined," and explains that after honing, the inner surface achieves the necessary cross grinding for oil film adhesion and low-friction piston work. A close-up reveals the engine block’s clean, honed cylinder bores. The scene transitions to Sven Bruckschlegel, Head of Engine Design, wearing a black Brabus polo shirt, who inspects the engine block using a borescope connected to a Sony monitor. Sven's voice is heard speaking German, translated by the narrator, explaining, "Here we have a cylinder made from aluminum and silicon crystals, and now we're checking whether the silicon crystals are sufficiently exposed." The video opens with a close-up of a gray Sony monitor, branded 'BRABUS', displaying a blue-tinted, magnified view of a cylinder bore's textured interior, likely from a borescope inspection. A hand enters the frame, pointing at specific features on the screen, indicating a detailed analysis of the surface finish. A male voice explains that the inspection focuses on the aluminum and silicon crystals within the bore, detailing how the harder silicon crystals are exposed to prevent piston rings from jamming and to allow an oil film to form for optimal lubrication. He further explains that if the silicon relief were too shallow, the aluminum would need to be etched back to ensure pistons don't jam later. The clean, well-lit workshop environment with white walls and a wooden workbench is visible. The camera then focuses on the engine block, showing its multiple cylinder bores and intricate internal structure as it is being inspected. The scene shifts to a table where a row of shiny, precision-machined connecting rods and pistons are laid out. The male voice continues, explaining that these high-end connecting rods are manufactured on-site and, like the motor pistons, are weighed to a tenth of a gram. The technician carefully places a piston on the scale, records the weight, and marks it with a number, as the voice emphasizes that employees sort components by weight to ensure all 12 piston rod pairs have the same mass, which is crucial for smooth engine running. The environment remains consistent with the bright, organized workshop setting, highlighting the precision and attention to detail in high-performance engine assembly, all set to a backdrop of subtle background music.

The segment opens with a close-up of a technician's hands meticulously placing a piston onto a digital scale for weighing, then marking it with a red marker. The scene expands to a clean, well-lit industrial workshop, where a male technician in a black Brabus-branded polo shirt and a female technician in a similar uniform are engaged in precision work. The male technician gestures towards a large, polished crankshaft mounted on a stand, while the female technician marks a connecting rod with a red marker. A German voice, likely the male technician, explains that this meticulous process ensures the 'perfect running of the high-performance engine,' distinguishing it from large-scale production with greater tolerances. He emphasizes that standard manufacturers aren't interested in the effort of weighing and matching every part, but here they 'figure out the smallest possible tolerance and then further re-work these parts,' calling it 'the best way for this kind of engine.' The voice details that "various load scenarios are run through at full throttle," with the technician altering the resistance of the dynamometer's rollers to create different test bench scenarios, including gradients and slopes. The segment concludes with a close-up of the Brabus's front wheel spinning rapidly on the dynamometer, showcasing its engineering prowess amidst the continuous engine sounds.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a well-lit testing facility, a black Brabus 900 Sedan is secured to a dynamometer by chains and cables, with an industrial fan positioned to simulate airflow. App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp, in the driver's seat, observes as a German voice explains the chassis dynamometer test aims to verify the vehicle's specified 800 horsepower performance, with the chains and cables preventing the car from moving forward. An industrial fan and blowers provide airflow to prevent the engine and cooling system from overheating, ensuring accurate measurements and avoiding engine damage. Close-ups reveal the car's wheels spinning and the dashboard displaying increasing speed and RPM, while an external monitor labeled 'Konstante Zugkraft' shows real-time torque, horsepower, and engine speed data. The fan spins rapidly, directing strong airflow at the car. The voice announces the next phase: accelerating from stationary to 300 kilometers per hour. The dashboard eventually registers 301 km/h and 1710 RPM, with boost pressure indicated, as the car accelerates from 0 to 185, highlighting precision engineering and high-tech performance testing.

Dynamometer.description: A performance testing machine, referred to as a 'chassis dynamometer' in the audio, with the Brabus 900 Sedan mounted on it, featuring a platform with chains and cables securing the car, and equipped with monitors displaying real-time engine output and vehicle performance.

Industrial Fan.description: A large, gray industrial fan with a protective cage, connected via a flexible duct, positioned in front of the car to simulate airflow. The audio explains its purpose is to 'provide a powerful wind and the supply of fresh air so that the engine and cooling system do not overheat during the sprint', preventing 'inaccurate measurement reading and possible engine damage'.

Chains and Cables.description: Orange and black chains and cables securing the Brabus 900 Sedan to the dynamometer platform. The audio clarifies they are used to 'securely anchor' the car to the floor, preventing the '800 horsepower vehicle' from going 'through the wall' during high-speed testing.

Dirk Berkenkopp.description: An App Developer seated in the driver’s seat of the Brabus 900 Sedan, wearing a black puffer jacket with red Brabus logos, focused on operating the vehicle during the dynamometer test.

The audio identifies it as an '800 horsepower vehicle' aiming to reach '300 kilometers per hour', with its wheels spinning and dashboard displaying real-time metrics up to 301 km/h.

raw_segments: The video opens inside a modern, well-lit testing facility, showcasing a sleek black Brabus 900 Sedan secured with orange and black chains and cables on a dynamometer. An industrial fan, connected via a flexible duct, is positioned to simulate airflow. Inside, App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp, wearing a black puffer jacket with red Brabus logos, is focused in the driver's seat. As the test begins, a voice explains in German, "We're looking to see if it actually attains the performance we specify here in the chassis dynamometer," specifically mentioning "800 horsepower." The audio continues, detailing how the chains and cables ensure the "800 horsepower vehicle" does "not go through the wall" by securely anchoring it to the floor. The large industrial fan and two blowers provide "a powerful wind and the supply of fresh air so that the engine and cooling system do not overheat during the sprint," preventing "inaccurate measurement reading and possible engine damage." Close-ups show the car's wheels spinning and the digital dashboard displaying rising speed and RPM, with a yellow and black color scheme. Outside, a monitor labeled 'Konstante Zugkraft' (Constant Traction) shows real-time performance data like torque, horsepower, and engine speed. The fan spins rapidly, directing strong airflow at the car. The voice then states, "Next we'll see if the car reaches its speed of 300 kilometers per hour by accelerating quickly to 300 from stationary." The dashboard eventually shows 301 km/h and 1710 RPM, along with boost pressure, as the car accelerates, with the audio noting "From 0 to 185". The scene emphasizes precision engineering and high-tech performance testing.

event_blocks.summary: The Brabus 900 Sedan successfully completed a high-speed dynamometer test, reaching 301 km/h with its engine roaring, confirmed by App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp and a narrator who noted the vehicle's flawless gear changes and overall performance. The test involved securing the car with chains and cables, simulating airflow with an industrial fan, and monitoring real-time data, with technicians testing up to four vehicles daily and prototypes undergoing up to 14 days of testing during development. Transitioning to the Brabus Classic Werk 4, a slower-paced environment dedicated to historic Mercedes-Benz vehicles, the facility functions as both a showroom and workshop, housing classic cars valued at approximately 20 million euros, including various 300 SL models. Technicians meticulously restore these vintage cars, aiming to return them to their original condition, a process that can take between 12 and 18 months and involves thousands of working hours. Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, inspects potential restoration candidates, emphasizing the importance of 'matching numbers' for original components like the engine, gearbox, and bonnet, and using a paint thickness gauge to assess body integrity, noting that accident-free vehicles with original welding points are crucial. Karl-Heinz Koch, Director of the Classic Workshop, oversees the detailed work on disassembled engine blocks, highlighting the 'handmade' nature of these vehicles and the necessity of traditional skills. This environment fosters a unique master-pupil dynamic, where experienced mechanics like Koch mentor younger trainees, such as Marc Schlechter, sharing knowledge and skills, exemplified by Koch learning from a South African trainee. The restoration process, described as 'six-star restoration,' meticulously reinstates every part, from chassis to cables, to original condition, with restored vehicles like the 280 SL Pagoda commanding prices exceeding 100,000 euros and a specific 300 SL priced at 1,890,000.00 EUR, underscoring the supreme discipline and particular skills required from Brabus Classic's select employees.

Dynamometer.description: A performance testing machine with the Brabus 900 Sedan mounted on it, featuring a platform with chains and cables securing the car, and equipped with monitors displaying real-time engine output and vehicle performance; described by a male narrator as a 'test bench' where prototypes can remain for up to 14 days.

The audio discusses the "global search for suitable base vehicles" for restoration, highlighting that these cars, despite being "past their best," are chosen for their "original features" and are subject to a meticulous "12 to 18 months" restoration process, resulting in a high market value, with a 280SL potentially reaching "nearly 300,000 Euros."

Dirk Berkenkopp.description: An App Developer seated in the driver’s seat of the Brabus 900 Sedan, wearing a black puffer jacket with red Brabus logos, focused on operating the vehicle during the dynamometer test.

Brabus 900 Sedan.description: A black luxury sedan with a prominent Brabus grille and 'BRABUS 900' badge, positioned on a dynamometer for performance testing, with its wheels spinning and dashboard displaying real-time metrics; successfully completed a high-speed test, reaching over 300 km/h, with smooth gear changes up to seventh gear, as confirmed by a male narrator.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of the Brabus 900 Sedan’s digital dashboard, displaying a speed of 301 km/h and other performance metrics during a high-speed dynamometer test, accompanied by an intense, driving musical score and the powerful roar of the engine. A male narrator's voice, speaking in German and translated, confirms the test's success, stating, 'The tester is satisfied. Time was good, so it completed the test without incident, changes gears without any problem, through all the gears up to seventh gear after 300 km/h, it's all good.' The camera cuts to App Developer Dirk Berkenkopp, focused in the driver’s seat, as the narrator adds, 'It passed the test.' We then see a monitor showing real-time performance data and the Brabus 900 Sedan on the dynamometer, its wheels spinning rapidly, secured by orange and black chains and cables, with an industrial fan simulating airflow. The narrator explains that 'The technician checks up to four vehicles every day' and 'In the development phase, a prototype is on the test bench for up to 14 days.' The scene transitions to an aerial shot of the large, modern Brabus Facility, a white-roofed building surrounded by greenery, then moves inside to the Brabus Classic Werk 4, where a more sophisticated musical theme begins. Here, the narrator explains, 'In contrast to Factory 3, there is a much slower pace in Factory 4. The Bottrop-based tuners also look after historic Mercedes-Benz vehicles.' The spacious, well-lit showroom is filled with meticulously maintained vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a silver 300SL and a black 190E, as the narrator identifies it as 'The Brabus Classic Department,' which is 'both a showroom and a workshop.' Technicians in Brabus-branded shirts are seen inspecting and working on these classic cars, with one on a hydraulic lift, as the narrator concludes, 'Here, the high-class vehicles are made to be as good as they were in their original condition. The restoration...'

The video opens in a well-lit workshop, accompanied by upbeat, sophisticated instrumental music, where a Technician in Black Shirt, wearing a black Brabus-branded polo shirt, is inspecting the undercarriage of a brown vintage Mercedes-Benz elevated on a Hydraulic Lift. He points upward, possibly discussing the vehicle’s condition with a man in a blue suit who gestures thoughtfully. The scene shifts to a workbench where the same technician handles a large, complex suspension component. A male voiceover explains that Brabus restorations include effective anti-corrosion protection and a two-year warranty with unlimited kilometers. The camera then pans to a pristine showroom labeled 'BRABUS Classic Werk 4', which the voiceover states houses classic cars with a total value of around 20 million euros, including several examples of the 300 SL series. A lineup of meticulously restored vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a vibrant orange, silver, and teal 300SL, are displayed under bright lighting, with close-ups highlighting chrome details, the iconic Mercedes-Benz grille, and the 'BRABUS Classic' badge. The voiceover specifically identifies the distinctive Gullwing from the 1950s as one of the most iconic vehicles, noting that restoration on one involves several thousand working hours and that these rare classics are sold for millions of euros. The camera focuses on a white Mercedes-Benz 300SL, with a Price Tag of 1,890,000.00 EUR visible on the windshield. The interior of a white classic car is shown, featuring a vintage dashboard with a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign, which the voiceover identifies as a 50-year-old Mercedes-Benz 280 SL Pagoda awaiting its revival, with restored Pagoda cars achieving market prices in excess of 100,000 euros. The man in the blue suit walks through the showroom, inspecting the vehicles closely and leaning into the white car to examine its interior. The voiceover then begins to mention "The technical manager pays a visit before every...", implying the technician's significant role. The environment is clean, spacious, and professional, emphasizing the luxury and precision of Brabus Classic’s restoration work.

Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, dressed in a blue suit and glasses, meticulously inspects a white vintage Mercedes-Benz 300SL with its hood open in a bright, clean workshop. As he gestures and points to various engine components, a narrator explains that he's looking for specific pointers because not every vehicle is suitable for the ambitious revitalization process. Gauffrés then speaks in German, translated by the narrator, acknowledging that 'at first sight, this car looks terrible,' but quickly asserts it has a 'sound basis for restoration' because it hasn't been in an accident and possesses 'matching numbers.' He points to a stamped number '6082' on the inner edge of the hood, emphasizing the significance of original components like the engine, gearbox, and bonnet. He further highlights the last digit of the chassis number etched into the bonnet, stressing the importance of these matching numbers for a car that is not only brilliantly restored but also retains its original integrity. The camera shows close-ups of the aged but well-preserved engine bay and the classic interior with analog gauges and wooden trim, where a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign rests on the windshield. The professional workshop, with large windows and a Brabus banner, underscores the high-end nature of the work. The narrator concludes by defining this meticulous process of reinstating old-timers to their original, as-new condition as 'six-star restoration' by the Bottrop team.

The video opens in the spacious, well-lit Brabus Classic Werk 4 showroom, displaying various vintage Mercedes-Benz models, including a silver 300SL and a black 190E. The focus shifts to a white vintage Mercedes-Benz 300SL with its hood open, revealing a detailed engine bay. Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, in a blue suit, inspects the car, while the audio states that "the vehicle is admittedly past its best, but it has the original features it had when it came off the production line." Gauffrés points to a stamped number '6082' on the inner hood and a 'Restaurationsfahrzeug' sign on the dashboard, as his voice highlights "the original welding points" as "a very good indicator that this car has definitely not been involved in an accident," calling it "a very important criterion." He then uses a handheld paint thickness gauge, displaying readings like '273' and '378', to inspect the car's body. The scene transitions to a well-lit, organized workshop where two technicians in Brabus-branded shirts work on a black vintage Mercedes-Benz; one is under the car on the suspension, while the other is at the engine bay. The narration explains that "the search for suitable base vehicles is a global one" and that "the luxury restoration then takes between 12 and 18 months," meticulously returning "chassis, engine, gearbox, and interior" – "every part, right up to the smallest piece of cable" – to its "original condition." The audio concludes by noting that "in the end, the 280SL will have a price tag of nearly 300,000 Euros," emphasizing that "the assembly of classic cars, faithful to the original, is a supreme discipline" demanding "very particular skills from the select employees," as "today, young mechatronics engineers often lack the necessary skill for this special art."

In a bright, clean Brabus workshop, Karl-Heinz Koch, Director of the Classic Workshop, is seen in a branded polo shirt, meticulously adjusting components on a disassembled engine block. The camera zooms in on the precision-machined cylinder bores and valve covers as Koch speaks, emphasizing that they work on "handmade" vehicles, where no two are alike, requiring a unique level of know-how that involves traditional skills like boring, sawing, and filing. Workshop sounds, including light clinking and tool use, underscore the activity. Other technicians in similar black Brabus shirts are busy with various engine parts, including a young trainee, Marc Schlechter, wearing blue nitrile gloves and a Brabus apron, assisting an older technician who uses a torque wrench. A narrator highlights that apprentices learn from 'old school chief vehicle mechanics' and that 'master and pupil benefit from each other.' Koch further elaborates on this dynamic, mentioning a technician (likely Marc Schlechter, the young trainee) who comes from South Africa and is learning German from him, while in return, he learns the tricks the younger technician has picked up over the years, describing it as a complementary relationship. The scene shifts to a brown vintage Mercedes-Benz elevated on a white hydraulic lift, where a man in a blue suit inspects it, as the narrator mentions 'the final inspection,' concluding a segment focused on professional, high-end classic car engineering and restoration.

event_blocks.summary: A brown vintage Mercedes-Benz, described as a "fully restored classic car" undergoing a "meticulous process" where "every detail needs to be perfect," is elevated on a white hydraulic lift, its undercarriage exposed, revealing gold-colored suspension parts, a central differential, and dual exhaust systems. Ulrich Joachim Gauffrés, CEO of the Technics Department, inspects the underside with a flashlight, emphasizing the precision required for "the color of the screws on the originally restored horn, the surface of the underseal, the color of the axle parts," and that "every cable must be laid correctly and be made with the original material." Gauffrés states that "when a car leaves here, we're entitled to say we have done everything, truly everything, right down to the final screw to present a perfect car." The scene transitions to the modern Brabus facility, "Factory 1," and then inside to a clean, well-lit workshop where a technician in a black Brabus shirt works on a gray Brabus G-Wagen. The technician "strips out everything from the vehicle's interior one piece at a time," removing the driver’s seat, disassembling the center console, and dismantling all the seats. Against a backdrop of instrumental, cinematic music, a technician pushes a cart loaded with black car seats to the upholstery shop, identified by a sign reading 'SATTLEREI,' where more than 20 specialists work. Inside the upholstery shop, technicians in black Brabus shirts meticulously stitch and assemble leather components, including elk, ostrich, mastic, or nubuck leather in around 3,500 shades, at white tables with industrial sewing machines, custom-making new seat covers, trims for dashboards, doors, headlining, the glove compartment, shelves, and a completely new floor. The video concludes by showcasing finished, high-end Brabus interiors: a luxurious tan leather cabin with reclining seats and a mini-bar, a modern white-and-black cockpit, and a sophisticated gray-and-black interior with Brabus-branded headrests.
